name: HHVM HHIR
summary: HHVM's region-scoped SSA IR: generated from a region (connected subgraph of HHBC), not whole functions. PHP/Hack dynamic dispatch, magic methods, weak typing, and reference semantics ride as HHIR-specific ops. Side-exit metadata maps back to HHBC offsets.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: HHIR is region-scoped SSA: it's generated from a *region* (a connected subgraph of HHBC, HHVM's bytecode) rather than from whole functions. Side-exit metadata maps back to HHBC offsets so failed speculations resume in the interpreter or baseline JIT. PHP/Hack dynamic dispatch, magic methods, weak typing, and reference semantics ride as HHIR-specific ops.

sections.title: Region vs whole-function JIT
sections.content:
Region-JIT orchestration takes a connected subgraph of bytecode (a hot path through a function or across function calls) rather than a single function. The benefit on a dynamic-dispatch language like PHP/Hack is that warm paths can be compiled without paying for the cold parts of the same function — magic-method bookkeeping, infrequent dispatch branches, and rarely-taken error paths stay interpreted.

The trade-off is more bookkeeping at side exits: each region has its own deopt boundary, and falling out of a hot region means resuming in the interpreter at a specific HHBC offset.
